name: Al Casey
abstract: Albert Aloysius Casey (September 15, 1915 – September 11, 2005) was an American jazz guitarist who was a member of Fats Waller's band during the 1930s and early 1940s.

concerts.label: 50th Anniversary of Benny Goodman's 1938 Carnegie Hall Concert

about_quotes.speaker: Vincent Pelote
about_quotes.evidence: I said yea, I like Wes. Al Casey is more approachable. I can copy him better... So I fell in love with the Al Casey album first.
about_quotes.doc_title: Vincent Pelote interviewed by Monk Rowe, Orlando, Florida, February 25, 2017

about_quotes.speaker: John Levy
about_quotes.evidence: Of course I did some sessions, like with Al Casey. Denzil and I worked with Al Casey, did some gigs with him.
about_quotes.doc_title: John Levy — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Teddy Wilson
about_quotes.evidence: my guitar player was one of the best that's ever been, Al Casey.

about_quotes.speaker: Jackie Williams
about_quotes.evidence: Al Casey and Butler, Jimmy Butler. They were the two of them and the drum... it was his idea to put us together. So they said well let’s get this, that was his idea to put us, the three of us together.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jackie Williams interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, October 18, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Ed Polcer
about_quotes.evidence: I would see Al Casey up on St. Nicholas Avenue, I’d hire him on a job and take him and wait while he brought his guitar up and came back for his amp.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ed Polcer interviewed by Monk Rowe, Tarrytown, New York, October 31, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: William Randolph "Cozy" Cole
about_quotes.evidence: In 1939 it was Frankie, Mezz Mezzrow ... Pete Brown, James P. Johnson, Al Casey, John Kirby and you. Do you remember those sessions at all?
